revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:官職]][[ko:관직]][[zh:职官]]


'''Kan'i''' (官職) refers to the roles and responsibilities of {{ill|officials|ja|官吏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|1=government official|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8985067}}. Specifically, it is categorized as follows, and the titles of such roles are called '''Kanmei''' (官名).

# '''Kan''' and '''Shoku''': "Kan" refers to the general type of duties, while "Shoku" indicates the specific scope of responsibilities.
# Positions assigned to {{ill|national civil servants|ja|国家公務員|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national civil servants|1=national public employee|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420729}}. In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, this term is primarily used for full-time and part-time employees in the {{ill|Ministries of Japan|en|Ministries of Japan|qid=Q15409298|lt=Executive branch of Japan|1=Ministries of Japan}} and the judicial branch. It is less commonly applied in the legislative branch.
# The general categorization of responsibilities handled by officials and their specific classifications below.
# The official position in administrative systems.

== Overview ==
As described above, Kan'i refers to the combination of "Kan" and "Shoku." Tracing its origin to historical texts of Japan and China, "Kan" in ancient China was defined in the {{ill|Shuowen Jiezi|ja|説文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuowen Jiezi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 説文解字|1=Shuowen Jiezi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1072348}} as "officials serving the ruler" and in the {{ill|Rites of Zhou|en|Rites of Zhou|qid=Q1367984|lt=Rites of Zhou|1=Rites of Zhou}} as "placing subordinates to govern a household." On the other hand, "Shoku" was defined in {{ill|Zengyun|ja|増韻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zengyun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as "a task in charge" and in the {{ill|Erya|ja|爾雅釈詁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Erya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as "the main responsibility," indicating that "Kan" and "Shoku" were considered separately.

Japan adopted the concept of Kan'i from the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|qid=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty|1=Sui dynasty}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} as part of its {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}. According to the {{ill|Gishi|ja|官位令義解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Kan refers to all officials from {{ill|Minister (government)|en|Minister (government)|qid=Q83307|lt=Minister|1=Minister (government)}} to scribes," and the {{ill|Shokuinryō Gishi|ja|職員令義解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuinryō Gishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specifies, "Shoku means administrative offices." However, in the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|qid=Q2702542|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, positions such as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}} and {{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|qid=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left|1=Minister of the Left}} were categorized under job titles, while various government departments were placed under office titles, indicating inconsistency in classification.

From the medieval period onward, a theory emerged distinguishing "Shoku" as roles in the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}}, while "Yaku" referred to roles in the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shogunate|qid=Q1074150}}. However, this distinction was not definitive.<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideyasu Wada |title=New Annotated Kan'i Manual |publisher=Kodansha |year=1983}}</ref>

The term "Shokusei" (職制) is also used to describe administrative systems related to Kan'i. Before World War II, the scholar {{ill|Kiyokazu Komonmura|ja|小中村清矩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyokazu Komonmura|1=Konakamura Kiyonori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6536303}} argued that roles defined by the Emperor were "Kan'i," while those established independently by the Shogunate were "Shokusei," distinguishing them on the basis of national legitimacy. This notion was later criticized in postwar Japan by scholars like Masajirō Takigawa.

Historically and today, being appointed to an official position is called {{ill|Ninkan|ja|任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|1=ninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11267917}}. Resigning from such a role was referred to as {{ill|Jikan|ja|辞官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in earlier times and is now generally termed as retirement ({{ill|Taikan|ja|退官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikan|1=Q11639302|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639302}}). However, the term Taikan remains legally used for judges under the Constitution. Dismissal from an official role is called {{ill|Minkan|ja|免官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 免職|1=dismissal from office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11388616}}. In feudal societies, corrupt practices like bribery to secure such positions were known as {{ill|Venal office|en|Venal office|qid=Q255077|lt=Baikan|1=Venal office}} or {{ill|Spoils system|en|Spoils system|qid=Q781376|lt=Ryōkan|1=Spoils system}}.

Today, the term Kan'i is commonly applied to roles of national civil servants. For example, the designation of part-time civil servants often includes the official title and Rank displayed as the recruitment position.

In the case of {{ill|National University Corporation|en|National University Corporation|qid=Q16141747|lt=National University Corporations|1=National University Corporation}} in Japan, senior staff appointments are often handled by the {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|qid=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}}, leading to the term "transferable Kan'i" being applied.<ref>{{Cite book |author=Ikufumi Amano |title=The Reality and Challenges of National Universities Post-Corporatization |year=2007 |publisher=National University Finance and Management Center}}</ref>

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further reading ==
* Ikufumi Amano, "The Reality and Challenges of National Universities Post-Corporatization," National University Finance and Management Center, 2007.
* Hideyasu Wada, annotated by Isao Tokoro, "New Annotated Kan'i Manual," Kodansha, 1983, ISBN 4061586211.

== See also ==

* {{ill|Revised Tang-style titles in Japan|ja|官職の唐風改称|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revised Tang-style titles in Japan|1=Revised Tang-style titles in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452124}}
* {{ill|Venal office|en|Venal office|qid=Q255077|lt=Baikan|1=Venal office}} / {{ill|Nankan|ja|年官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nankan|1=Nengan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483830}}
* {{ill|Ninkan|ja|任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|1=ninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11267917}} / {{ill|Taikan|ja|退官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikan|1=Q11639302|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639302}}
* {{ill|National civil servant|ja|国家公務員|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National civil servant|1=national public employee|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420729}} / {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|qid=Q572700|lt=Bureaucracy|1=Bureaucrat}} / {{ill|Public service|ja|公職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public service|1=Public office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q294414}}
* {{ill|Shinninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinninkan|qq=}} / {{ill|Chokunin|ja|勅任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokunin|1=chokuninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11399919}} / {{ill|Sōnin-kan|ja|奏任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōnin-kan|1=sōninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11445526}} / {{ill|Hannin-kan|ja|判任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hannin-kan|1=hanninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11396615}}
* {{ill|Higher official Ranks of Japan|ja|高等官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higher official Ranks of Japan|1=Kōtōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11672687}}
* {{ill|Bureaucracy|en|Bureaucracy|qid=Q72468|lt=Bureaucracy|1=Bureaucracy}}
* {{ill|Spoils system|en|Spoils system|qid=Q781376|lt=Ryōkan|1=Spoils system}}
* {{ill|Samurai court titles|ja|武家官位|1=Buke-kani|1=Samurai Court Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai court titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545897}} / {{ill|Kantoshō|ja|官途状|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantoshō|1=kandojō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452142}} / {{ill|Hyakkanmyō|ja|百官名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakkanmyō|1=Hyakkan-na|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580724}} / {{ill|Tōhyakkan|ja|東百官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōhyakkan|1=Azuma Hyakkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11528106}}
* {{ill|Kanshoku Hisan|ja|官職秘抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshoku Hisan|1=Kanshoku Hishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452127}}
* {{ill|Bunkan|ja|文官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkan|1=civilian official|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16919908}}
* {{ill|Gikan|ja|技官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gikan|1=Technical Official|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11497342}}
